title: 2023 Census population counts (by ethnic group, age, and Māori descent) and dwelling counts | Stats NZ
content:
## 2023 Census population counts (by ethnic group, age, and Māori descent) and dwelling counts
29 May 2024, 10:45am
2023 Census population and dwelling counts provide counts of people (by ethnic group, age, and Māori descent) and dwellings for New Zealand and geographic areas.
- 2023 Census national and subnational usually resident population counts and dwelling counts Excel spreadsheet, 330 KB

### 2023 Census national and subnational usually resident population counts and dwelling counts
Excel spreadsheet, 330 KB
Since the 2018 Census:
- New Zealand’s population has grown by almost 300,000 people
- three out of four New Zealanders live in the North Island
- Selwyn and Queenstown are still the fastest growing territorial authorities
- almost 1 million New Zealanders were of Māori descent at the time of the 2023 Census
- the population continues to age
- the population continues to diversify.

## Growth in New Zealand’s population since 2018 Census similar to adding current census populations of Northland and Southland regions
New Zealand’s 35th Census of Population and Dwellings was held on 7 March 2023 and showed that the 2023 Census usually resident population (CURP) count was 4,993,923 – up 294,168 (6.3 percent) from the 2018 Census (4,699,755).
- Between 2018 and 2023 the population grew 6.3 percent, a slower rate than between 2013 and 2018, when the population grew by 10.8 percent.
- The total dwelling count in the 2023 Census increased by 170,061 (9.0 percent) to 2,056,578, up from 1,886,517 in the 2018 Census.
Census usually resident population count, 1981−2023 Censuses

## Three out of four New Zealanders live in the North Island
While the North Island had the larger population increase (growing by 213,453 people compared with 80,745 people in the South Island), the South Island population had a higher rate of increase (growing by 7.3 percent, compared with 5.9 percent in the North Island).
- The fastest growing regions in the South Island were Tasman (10.3 percent increase, from 52,389 to 57,807) and Canterbury (8.6 percent increase, from 599,694 to 651,027).
- Auckland remained the most populous region with nearly 1.66 million people, although its growth rate halved between 2018 and 2023 compared with the previous intercensal period. The growth rate increase was down from 11.0 percent between 2013 and 2018, to 5.4 percent between 2018 and 2023.
- The regions neighbouring Auckland had the highest growth rates in the North Island. Waikato’s population increased by 8.9 percent (from 458,202 in 2018 to 498,771 in 2023), and Northland’s by 8.3 percent (from 179,076 in 2018 to 194,007 in 2023).
- The lowest growth rates were recorded in Southland (2.7 percent increase from 97,467 to 100,143), and Wellington (2.8 percent increase from 506,814 to 520,971).
Text alternative for map, Percentage change in the census usually resident population count between 2018 and 2023 by regional council
Map of New Zealand shows the percentage change in the census usually resident population count for the regional council areas, between the 2018 and 2023 Censuses. Change for Northland was more than 8.0 percent; Auckland 4.0 to 5.9 percent; Waikato 8.0 percent or more; Bay of Plenty 8.0 percent or more; Gisborne 6.0 to 7.9 percent; Hawke’s Bay 4.0 to 5.9 percent; Taranaki 6.0 to 7.9 percent; Manawatū-Whanganui 4.0 to 5.9 percent; Wellington 0.0 to 3.9 percent; Tasman 8.0 percent or more; Nelson 0.0 to 3.9 percent; Marlborough 4.0 to 5.9 percent; West Coast 4.0 to 5.9 percent; Canterbury 8.0 percent or more; Otago 6.0 to 7.9 percent; Southland 0.0 to 3.9 percent.

## Selwyn and Queenstown still the fastest growing territorial authorities
Almost 80,000 people (78,144) called Selwyn district home at the time of the 2023 Census, compared with 60,561 people in 2018 and 44,595 in 2013. This represented a 29.0 percent increase since 2018 Census, compared with 35.8 percent between 2013 and 2018.
- The Queenstown-Lakes district population increased 22.1 percent to reach 47,808 people in 2023 (up from 39,153 in 2018). However, this was lower than the increase between 2013 and 2018 when the population grew by 38.7 percent (from 28,224 to 39,153).
- All territorial authority areas increased in population except Wellington city and Chatham Islands territory (from 202,737 in 2018 to 202,689 in 2023, and from 663 in 2018 to 612 in 2023, respectively).
- Auckland’s most populous local board area, Howick, gained 12,600 new usual residents (8.9 percent increase since 2018), while the largest increase in population occurred in Papakura, with 14,682 new usual residents (25.5 percent increase since 2018).

## Almost 1 million New Zealanders of Māori descent at the 2023 Census
The census provides counts of Māori in two ways: Māori descent and Māori ethnicity. The two concepts are closely related but not the same.
Māori descent is based on whakapapa while affiliation to the Māori ethnic group is a self-determined cultural affiliation.
Māori descent – 2023 Census: Information by concept – DataInfo+ Māori descent provides more information.
- the Māori descent census usually resident population count increased by 12.5 percent to 978,246, compared with 869,850 in 2018 Census
- people of Māori descent made up 19.6 percent of the total population, up from 18.5 percent in 2018
- Auckland region still has the largest Māori descent population nationally, at 227,898, up from 207,183 in 2018
- in Gisborne region over half the population were of Māori descent, increasing from 54.6 percent in 2018 to 56.0 in 2023, which was the highest proportion nationally. Otago and Tasman regions had the lowest proportions with 11.6 and 11.8 percent.

## New Zealand’s population continues to age
In 2023, the median age of the population increased to 38.1 years, compared with 37.4 years in the 2018 Census (half the population are younger, and half older than this age).
- Auckland and Gisborne regions had the youngest median age, with 35.9 years and 36.7 years respectively in 2023, and West Coast and Tasman regions had the oldest median age, with 48.1 years and 46.8 years respectively.
- While overall there was an increase in median age, three regions showed a trend towards a younger population, with Hawke’s Bay (from 40.6 years in 2018, to 40.4 years in 2023), Gisborne (from 37.0 years to 36.7 years), and Bay of Plenty (from 40.2 years to 39.7 years) all recording decreases in median age.
- The median age of the Māori descent usually resident population increased to 27.2 years, from 26.0 years in the 2018 Census.

## New Zealand’s population continues to diversify
While the population identifying with European ethnicities remains the largest at nearly 3.4 million (3,383,742 people, or 67.8 percent), people identifying with Māori, Asian, Pacific peoples, and Middle Eastern/Latin American/African (MELAA) ethnicities increased their share of the total population.
In the 2023 Census we counted:
- 887,493 people (17.8 percent) as Māori
- 861,576 people (17.3 percent) as Asian
- 442,632 people (8.9 percent) as Pacific peoples
- 92,760 people (1.9 percent) as MELAA
- the Asian and Māori ethnic groups had the largest numeric increases between the 2018 and 2023 Censuses, up 153,978 people and 111,657 people respectively.
Ethnicity – 2023 Census: Information by concept – DataInfo+ provides more information.

### 2023 Census maps and data
2023 Census maps and data for interactive maps.

### Population estimates
Estimates of the resident population (ERP) give the best measure of the population living in each area of New Zealand. They are based on the census usually resident population counts but are higher, because the estimates make an allowance for net census undercount and residents who are temporarily overseas at the time of the census.
Population estimates are updated annually (at 30 June) for local areas to give an indication of population change since the last census. National population estimates are updated quarterly. The estimates are updated for births, deaths, and net migration (external and internal) since the last estimate.
Population estimates based on the 2023 Census, including revised estimates between 2018 and 2023, will be available in early 2025. These will incorporate estimates of net census undercount from the 2023 Post-enumeration Survey, scheduled for release on 9 December 2024.
Population statistics – user guide and Subnational population estimates (TA, SA2), by age and sex, at 30 June 1996-2023 (2023 boundaries) in NZ.Stat provide more information.

2023 Census of Population and Dwellings − DataInfo+ provides information about methods, and related metadata.
Data quality ratings for 2023 Census variables provides information about the variables and their quality.
